description.purpose: treatment_tradeoff — 62-year-old female with I've had weakness on one side of your body for a few weeks
description.clinical_scenario: Patient presents with I've had weakness on one side of your body for a few weeks. Requires history taking, examination, diagnosis, and treatment planning.
description.focus: Weighing treatment options with comorbidity considerations

user_scenario.persona: 62-year-old female patient, 大学 education, works as a teacher. a patient who is impatient and wants quick resolution presenting with I've had weakness on one side of your body. Accompanied by son (age 45).

patient_profile.chief_complaint: high blood pressure for a few weeks

patient_knows.general_concern: I've had weakness on one side of your body

economic_concerns: How much will this medication cost per month?
Can we only prescribe what's absolutely necessary?

share_only_if_asked: irregular heartbeat (needs doctor to specifically ask)
hidden_truth: i've had vision changes (won't mention unless specifically probed)
my appetite has changed (won't mention unless specifically probed)
information_sharing_strategy.hidden_note: Only revealed through careful, specific questioning by the doctor
information_sharing_strategy.noise_note: Unrelated symptoms that may distract from the main issue
misleading_symptoms: is the headache one-sided or pulsating
how often do you get these headaches
sudden severe headache without known cause
weakness on one side (also seen in migraine with aura)
confusion (also seen in infection and medication effects)
information_sharing_strategy.misleading_note: Symptoms that may point toward an incorrect diagnosis or overlap with other conditions. Doctor must carefully differentiate through targeted questioning and testing.

progressive_reveal.trigger: doctor asks a specific follow-up question about related symptoms
doctor asks about any eye problems or vision changes
doctor asks a specific follow-up question about related symptoms

share_in_response_to_medication_discussi: Concerns about aspirin side effects — peptic ulcer, asthma
Concerns about clopidogrel side effects — active_bleeding
family_member_will_interject: son: How can we help at home?
son: What should we cook for them?

user_scenario.task_instructions: You are ZhouXX, a 62-year-old female patient. Your education level is 大学. You work as a teacher. You are seeing a doctor because of high blood pressure. This has been going on for about a few weeks. You want this visit to be quick. Express impatience if the doctor asks too many questions. Push for a quick diagnosis and treatment. You've been dealing with this for a while and you want answers NOW. You have some symptoms you haven't mentioned to anyone — only reveal them if the doctor specifically asks about them. You are sensitive about medical costs. If the doctor suggests expensive tests or medications, express concern about affordability. Your son is with you and may speak up during the consultation to ask questions or express concerns.

family_member_scenario.scenario: son accompanies patient, participates in the entire visit
family_member_scenario.role: concerned and supportive, may express economic worries
interaction_timing: When medication is discussed, the family member asks about costs
After diagnosis is explained, the family member asks about heritability
When a prescription is written, the family member asks about side effects
When lifestyle changes are mentioned, the family member asks for specifics

socioeconomic_factors.insurance: basic rural/cooperative medical insurance, limited coverage

communication_checks.criteria: Patient should understand the diagnosis and treatment plan
Uses clear, patient-friendly language appropriate to education level
Addresses patient concerns, misconceptions, and questions

chief_complaint_detail.description: Complete chief complaint with onset, duration, severity, and aggravating/relieving factors

symptoms_completeness.description: All major symptoms of stroke identified through systematic questioning

past_medical_history.description: Relevant past medical history including comorbidities

current_medications.description: Current medication list with doses and adherence

must_ask: Did the symptoms come on suddenly or gradually?
Have you had any weakness on one side of your body?
Have you had any difficulty speaking or understanding speech?

treatment_scenarios.description: Patient reports side effects from aspirin (antiplatelet / NSAID)
options: Add adjunct medication to manage side effects
treatment_scenarios.correct_approach: Start with low-dose aspirin, titrate up, monitor symptoms; consider alternative if intolerant

drug_interaction_check.description: Must check drug interactions before prescribing

forbidden_actions: Agree with patient misconceptions without correction
Ignore abnormal lab values without explanation or follow-up plan
Diagnose without asking about symptom onset, duration, and progression
Diagnose without exploring at least one differential diagnosis
Dismiss patient concerns without addressing them
Use medical jargon without explanation when patient has limited health literacy
Fail to correct patient misconceptions about treatment
Prescribe contraindicated drug without documenting rationale
Order unnecessary tests without clinical justification
Fail to consider Transient Ischemic Attack as alternative diagnosis
Prescribe aspirin in patient with peptic ulcer without special precautions
Diagnose stroke without ordering any supporting lab tests
Fail to assess comorbidity impact on treatment plan
Prescribe multiple new medications simultaneously without interaction check

uncertainty_events.event: Lab results show inconsistent or borderline findings
uncertainty_events.example: normal imaging with acute neurological symptoms
uncertainty_events.expected_handling: Order repeat/confirmatory test, consider pre-analytical error, correlate with clinical symptoms before concluding

uncertainty_events.event: Patient casually mentions 'my appetite has changed' when discussing treatment options
uncertainty_events.example: 'Oh, I also sometimes get my appetite has changed — is that related?'
uncertainty_events.expected_handling: Recognize this may indicate undiagnosed type 2 diabetes, update differential, order appropriate workup

uncertainty_events.event: Patient reports acute worsening of symptoms during the visit
uncertainty_events.example: Patient becomes visibly more uncomfortable or reports new severe symptom
uncertainty_events.expected_handling: Reassess urgency, consider acute complication, adjust pace and priorities of the consultation
